content: Hey Saturday, so I've decided to finally build that workshop setup in my garage. I just picked up a used DeWalt table saw and a Bosch router, and I want to get everything organized before summer when my partner wants to use the garage for parking during the hot months. Do you think I should start by building a workbench first, or should I focus on getting the tools set up properly?

That's a solid project! Starting with a workbench is definitely the right call—it'll give you a stable platform for assembly and it'll make using those other tools safer and more efficient. A good workbench will actually help you set up and tune that table saw, which is important for accuracy. I'd suggest building the bench first (maybe 2-3 weeks of casual work), then mounting or organizing your tools around it. Since you've got a deadline before summer, I'd prioritize a functional 4x2 foot bench with a vise—nothing too fancy, but rigid enough for routing and saw work. How much garage space are we talking about, and do you already have materials lined up?

I've got maybe 12x10 feet of usable space after parking one car and leaving room for the garage door to open. I haven't gotten materials yet—I was planning to hit up the local Lowe's this weekend. What wood should I use for the top? I've seen people use everything from pine to laminated hardwood, and I'm not sure if it's worth spending extra on something like maple.

For a workbench, you've got good options depending on your budget and what you'll be doing. Pine or construction-grade lumber is actually fine for the frame and legs—it's cheap and strong enough. For the top, hardwood like hard maple is nice because it's durable and doesn't dent as easily, but honestly, laminated pine or even MDF with a hardwood edge works great if you're budget-conscious. Since you're mainly doing woodworking with a table saw and router, I'd say go with a 2.5 to 3-inch thick top using laminated 2x6 pine boards—it'll cost maybe $150-200 for the whole workbench and still give you the rigidity you need. Avoid the fancy stuff for now; you can always upgrade later. How's your tool collection looking beyond the saw and router—do you have a miter saw or circular saw for rough cuts?

I've got an old circular saw my dad gave me years ago, and I have a basic miter saw that I picked up at a garage sale maybe three years ago. Not the fanciest stuff but it works. I also have a drill, some hand tools, and a few clamps. I'm a bit concerned about dust management though—my partner really doesn't want the whole garage caked in sawdust before summer. Should I invest in a dust collector?

Dust management is smart thinking, especially with the summer deadline and your partner's concerns. For a small garage workshop, I wouldn't jump straight to a full dust collection system—those can be expensive ($300+) and overkill for occasional use. Instead, start with shop vacuum attachment for your table saw and router (most newer models have ports), and keep a basic Shop-Vac handy. A 5-gallon Shop-Vac runs about $40-60 and is perfect for a one-car garage operation. Add a dust mask ($15 for good ones), and you're golden. You could also run a temporary poly curtain around your work area if dust is really a problem. The truth is, you'll control most dust by sealing edges with a vacuum attachment rather than buying expensive infrastructure right away. Focus on the bench first, dust solution second.

That makes sense. So here's a timeline question—if I start building the bench this weekend, realistically how long do you think it'll take to get it fully functional? And should I build a tool storage cabinet at the same time, or would that stretch the timeline too much? My partner mentioned she wants everything wrapped up by late May, and it's already mid-March.

Okay, so you've got about 10 weeks, which is actually pretty reasonable. A basic workbench—cutting materials, assembly, finishing—should take 3-4 weekends of casual work if you're working maybe 4-6 hours each time. Getting it mounted and solid with a vise adds another weekend or two. I'd say get the bench fully done and dialed in by early April, then you've got plenty of time for a simple tool storage solution. For storage, I wouldn't build an elaborate cabinet right away—just get some wall-mounted pegboard or French cleats above the bench ($30-50 for materials), and use that for your router, power tools, and hand tools. You could add a proper cabinet later if you want. This keeps the scope manageable while still getting everything organized before summer. Does your partner have specific concerns about the space, or is it mainly the dust and wanting it to look somewhat organized?

She just wants to be able to actually park in the garage again without worrying about getting sawdust on the car or tripping over tools. I think the pegboard solution sounds perfect for that. One more thing—I've been watching some YouTube videos on table saw safety, and I'm getting a little nervous about using it. Should I look into a fence upgrade or aftermarket blade before I even start the workbench, or is the stock DeWalt fence okay to start with?

Smart thinking on the safety angle—table saws deserve respect. The stock DeWalt fence is actually decent and will work fine for your workbench project and general cutting. Don't feel like you need to upgrade immediately. What I'd recommend instead is investing in good safety accessories: a blade guard, featherboards, and push sticks (these cost maybe $40-60 total and honestly save lives). Watch some Norm Abrams or Steve Ramsey videos on proper technique—they're the gold standard for safe practices. Get yourself a good ear and eye protection while you're at it. Once you've made a few cuts and feel comfortable, *then* think about upgrading the fence if you want. For now, the stock fence is reliable enough, and proper technique matters way more than fancy equipment. When are you planning to actually make your first cuts?

I'm thinking this Saturday I'll pick up the lumber at Lowe's, maybe spend Sunday doing the layout and cutting. Does that sound reasonable? Also, what's the best way to finish the workbench top—should I seal it or just leave it natural? I want something that's easy to clean but doesn't require constant maintenance.

Saturday lumber run + Sunday cutting is perfect timing. Just give yourself maybe 4-5 hours on Sunday—don't try to rush through your first major table saw use. Take breaks, stay focused. For the finish, I'd recommend a simple approach: light sand everything smooth (120 grit), then just apply a clear polyurethane or Helmsman spar urethane in a matte finish. Two coats takes maybe an hour of actual work spread over two evenings, and it makes cleanup way easier (sawdust doesn't stick to sealed wood). Skip the exotic stuff—you want practical, not pretty. The sealed finish will handle occasional spills and is durable enough for workshop abuse. Matte looks better in a garage anyway, and it won't be slippery like gloss. Before you go Saturday, grab a workbench book or find some detailed plans online—I'd recommend looking up 'Roubo workbench plans' or 'Roy Underhill workbench'—they're solid, time-tested designs. Want me to help you think through the cutting list?

Yes, definitely! I want to do a 48 inches wide by 24 inches deep bench with a 4x4 leg design and a vise on the left side. Can you help me figure out the cutting list? I'm assuming I'll need 2x6 lumber for the top, but I'm fuzzy on the frame dimensions and how many legs I actually need. This is where I realize I'm pretty much a beginner at planning this stuff.

No worries—this is exactly what planning is for! Here's a simple cutting list for your 48x24 bench: For the top, get six 2x6x8 boards (you'll cut them to 48 inches and laminate them together for a solid 3-inch thick top). For the frame, use 2x4s: four pieces at 24 inches for the short rails (front and back, two per side), four pieces at 36 inches for the long rails (front and back, two per side), and four 4x4 legs cut at around 32-34 inches tall depending on your height (you want the bench about 34-36 inches high). That gives you a sturdy rectangular frame you can bolt together, then mount the top. You'll want at least four legs for stability, positioned near the corners. For the vise, you'll either mount it to one of the front rails or directly to a leg on the left side—most people use a woodworking vise like a Bessey or similar. Get 3/8-inch bolts and washers for assembly instead of screws—much more rigid. Should I walk you through the assembly order next, or do you want to grab the materials first?

Actually, one question before I buy—how much is this going to cost in total? I want to make sure I'm not blowing the budget without realizing it. I'm thinking maybe $300-400 total for the whole bench including the vise? Is that realistic or am I being too optimistic here?

Your budget is actually pretty realistic if you're smart about it. Let's break it down: six 2x6x8 pine boards ($15 each = $90), 2x4 lumber for the frame ($50-60), 4x4 legs ($40-50), bolts and hardware ($25-30), and a basic woodworking vise ($60-100 for a decent used one, or $120-150 new). Finish and sandpaper ($20), pegboard and brackets ($40)—you're looking at around $350-400 if you buy new. You can save money by hunting for a used vise on Facebook Marketplace or Craigslist (that can drop $50-100 easily), or by getting your lumber from a salvage yard. If you're flexible on the vise and willing to DIY one temporarily, you could come in under $300. I'd suggest not cheaping out on the vise though—it's the one thing you'll use constantly and want to work smoothly. Check Craigslist first for a used Bessey or equivalent before buying new. Does $350-400 work with your budget?

Yeah, that's within my range. Okay, I think I'm ready to go this Saturday. One last thing—my circular saw is pretty old and I'm a bit worried about using it for ripping the 2x6 boards. Should I just make all the cuts with the table saw instead, or is there a reason to bring the circular saw into this? I want to make sure my first cuts are solid.

For your first major cuts, stick with the table saw—it's way safer and more accurate for ripping long boards, which is what you're doing with those 2x6s. The circular saw is better for crosscutting and has its place, but table saw gives you a guide fence and cleaner rips. Since you're still getting comfortable with the tool, having that fence to rely on is huge. Your old circular saw is better saved for future projects anyway. Set up the table saw properly (check that the blade is square to the table, make sure the fence is parallel to the blade), do a test cut on scrap wood first, then tackle your 2x6s slowly and carefully. Take your time—this isn't a race. And definitely use a push stick once your hands get within 6 inches of the blade. How are you feeling about the whole thing now? Any other concerns before Saturday?

Honestly, I feel pretty good about it now. I think talking through the whole thing has made it less intimidating. My main goal is just to have a functional workspace by late May, and it sounds like the workbench is totally doable in that timeframe. I might check Craigslist for a used vise tonight, and then I'm ready to hit Lowe's Saturday morning. Should I follow up with you after the first cuts to make sure I'm doing things right?

Absolutely, please do! I'd love to hear how the cuts went, what you learned, and if you run into any snags. That's actually super valuable feedback for planning the next phases. After you get the frame built and the top laminated, we can talk about mounting and finishing, then move on to the vise installation. And once the bench is solid, we can strategize the pegboard setup and figure out how to organize your table saw and router so everything's accessible but safe. You've got a solid plan, good timeline, and realistic expectations—that's honestly the hardest part of any project. Good luck Saturday, take your time with those first cuts, and definitely send me a quick update when you've made some progress. You're going to do great!
